entries.title: Markarian's Chain
entries.description:
Near the heart of the
Virgo Galaxy
Cluster,
a string of galaxies known as 
Markarian's
Chain stretches across this
telescopic field of view

entries.title: Seeing Titan
entries.description:
Shrouded in a thick atmosphere, the surface of
Saturn's largest moon, Titan,
is really hard to see

entries.title: Trifid Pillars and Jets
entries.description:
Dust pillars are like 
interstellar mountains

entries.title: Orion over Mount Teide
entries.description: Orion is rarely seen like this

entries.title: The Retrograde Dance of Saturn and Neptune
entries.description:
What does it mean for 
Saturn and 
Neptune to be in retrograde? 
Featured is a composite of images taken over 34 nights from May 2025 to February 
2026 tracing Saturn (brighter, foreground) and Neptune (dimmer, background)

entries.title: Comet R3 PanSTARRS Before Rigel
entries.description:
Which way is 
Comet R3 PanSTARRS going?

Not towards the star at the top of the image, because that is 
Rigel, which, being far in the background, 
is 
unrelated to the comet

entries.title: Messier Craters in Stereo
entries.description:
Many bright nebulae and star clusters in planet Earth's sky
are associated with the name of
astronomer Charles Messier
from his famous 18th century catalog

entries.title: Comet R3 PanSTARRS and Orion
entries.description: Orion never had a sword like this

entries.title: Moon Setting Behind Teide Volcano
entries.description: These people are not in danger

entries.title: The Conjunction of Comet R3 PanSTARRS and the Orion Nebula
entries.description:
Today’s composite image 
features 
something old, 
something new, something borrowed, and something blue! 
Comet R3 PanSTARRS, 
streaking across the right of the image, likely originated from 
the Oort Cloud, 
meaning it is an  old  Solar System relic from billions of years ago

entries.title: NGC 188: Old Cluster in the New General Catalog
entries.description: The New General Catalog of star clusters and nebulae really isn't so new

entries.title: Messier Catalog at Uniform Scale
entries.description:
What are some of the most interesting astronomical objects you can see in the night sky?

    Armed with a good pair of binoculars or a small telescope, if you live in the Northern Hemisphere, you can look for the very popular objects in the Messier Catalog

entries.title: R3 PanSTARRS: An Orion Comet
entries.description: Comet R3 PanSTARRS might be best remembered as an Orion comet

entries.title: Aurora Slathers Up the Sky
entries.description:
Like salsa verde on your favorite burrito, a green
aurora
slathers up the sky in this 2017 June 25 snapshot from the
International Space Station

entries.title: NGC 1300: Barred Spiral Galaxy
entries.description:
Across the center of this spiral galaxy is a 
bar

entries.title: Unraveling NGC 3169
entries.description:
Spiral galaxy NGC 3169 looks to be unraveling like a ball of cosmic
yarn

entries.title: NGC 2170: The Angel Nebula
entries.description:
Is this a painting or a photograph?

In this celestial
abstract art 
composed with a cosmic brush, dusty nebula
NGC 2170,
also known as the 
Angel Nebula, shines just above the image center

entries.title: The Dark Wolf Nebula
entries.description: A dark wolf lies in gum

entries.title: A Collision of Galaxy Clusters
entries.description: This big beautiful spiral shines in X-ray light

entries.title: The Nebulous Realm of WR 134
entries.description:
This cosmic snapshot
covers a field of view over twice as wide as the full Moon within the
boundaries of the high-flying constellation Cygnus

entries.title: Messier 2
entries.description:
After the Crab Nebula,
this giant star cluster is the second entry in
18th century astronomer Charles Messier's famous list of
things that are not comets

entries.title: Thackeray's Globules
entries.description:
What are these strange space globs? 

Situated in 
rich star fields
and glowing hydrogen gas, these opaque clouds of 
interstellar dust 
and gas are so large they might be able to form stars

entries.title: NGC 3660 and Burçin's Galaxy
entries.description: The upper galaxy might be more photogenic, but the lower galaxy is more unusual

entries.title: PK 164 +31.1: The Headphone Nebula
entries.description:
What is a pair of headphones doing in the sky? Today’s image features the Headphone Nebula, also known as 
PK 164 +31

entries.title: NGC 1514: The Crystal Ball Nebula
entries.description:
What do you see in this crystal ball?

    The featured image shows NGC 1514, known as the Crystal Ball Nebula, observed by the Gemini North telescope on Maunakea, in Hawai'i

entries.title: Messier 104
entries.description:
A gorgeous spiral galaxy,
Messier 104 is famous
for its nearly edge-on
profile featuring a broad ring of obscuring dust lanes

entries.title: Supermoon Versus Micromoon
entries.description:
What is so micro about tonight's blue micromoon?  

Just after sunset, a 
full moon will appear 
slightly smaller and dimmer than usual

entries.title: Eagle Nebula Pillars in Infrared from Hubble
entries.description: Newborn stars are forming in the Eagle Nebula

entries.title: Saturn at Night
entries.description:
Telescopic views of Saturn and its beautiful rings
often make it the star of
star
parties

entries.title: The Vela Supernova Remnant
entries.description: The explosion is over, but the consequences continue

entries.title: Andromeda Through Gas and Dust
entries.description:
Over 1000 years ago, Persian astronomer 
Abd al-Rahman al-Sufi 
published humanity’s oldest known record of 
the Andromeda Galaxy 
in "The Book of Fixed Stars" 
(Bodleian Library MS

entries.title: A Planetary Nebula with Cosmic Buckyballs
entries.description:
What is happening inside this unusual nebula?

   Planetary nebula Tc 1, captured here in exquisite detail by the James Webb Space Telescope, is the celestial site where buckyballs were first identified in 2010

entries.title: The Hydra Cluster of Galaxies
entries.description:
Within our own Milky Way galaxy, two bright, spiky stars stand
like sentinels
in the foreground of
this cosmic snapshot

entries.title: Charon: Moon of Pluto
entries.description:
A darkened and mysterious north polar region
known to some as
Mordor Macula caps this premier view
of Charon, Pluto's largest moon

entries.title: Jupiter and Venus from Earth
entries.description: It was visible around the world

entries.title: Comet R3 PanSTARRS Through Time
entries.description:
What happens to a comet as it leaves our inner Solar System? 

Now, the arrival of a comet into the inner 
Solar System is typically heralded with great fanfare and high hopes that the comet will become 
bright and 
photogenic

entries.title: Thor's Helmet
entries.description:
Thor not only has 
his own day 
(Thursday), but a helmet in the heavens

entries.title: The Eagle Nebula and Friends
entries.description:
What looks as if it is going to swallow the great 
Pillars of Creation? 
The Eagle Nebula 
(M16) 
is not a bird, a plane, or 
Superman

entries.title: The Mermaid Nebula Supernova Remnant
entries.description:
Could the Little Mermaid turn into stardust instead of seafoam?

    It would seem so in this beautiful nebula

entries.title: NASA’s Career Technical Education Day Highlights Technical Careers
entries.description: At NASA, remaining a global leader in exploration and innovation includes having a skilledand dedicated workforce. Technicians play a critical role in advancing the agency’sresearch and missions, applying hands-on expertise across engineering, fabrication,electronics, and countless other technical fields. To help cultivate the next generation of technical talent, NASA’s Office of STEM Engagementhosted Career Technical Education […]

entries.title: Soccer Meets Space Science
entries.description: A soccer ball floats in microgravity in this March 2, 2026, picture from the International Space Station. The space station crew tested soccer balls to study how internal mass affects motion and stability in microgravity. The findings have improved understanding of how embedded technologies, including match-ball sensors, can influence performance during play. Through research aboard […]

entries.title: NASA Robotic Tech Demo Will Advance Prototype Gamma-Ray Detectors
entries.description: A new type of gamma-ray sensor developed by NASA will take part in a robotic arm demonstration on the agency’s upcoming Fly Foundational Robots mission.

entries.title: I Am Artemis: Elkin Norena
entries.description: Listen to this audio excerpt from Elkin Norena, resident management officer, NASA’s Space Launch System Program: NASA’s Elkin Norena has helped the agency launch more than a dozen space shuttle missions – that’s more than a dozen crews to low Earth orbit and more than a dozen historic missions. They were missions that helped build […]

entries.title: NASA’s Chandra Discovers Possible Supernova Remnant in Galactic Center
entries.description: Using data from NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory, astronomers may have found a supernova remnant in an intriguing neighborhood in the middle of our galaxy. A paper describing these new findings published in The Astrophysical Journal. Supernova remnants are the expanding remains of exploded stars and provide elements – like iron, oxygen, and silicon – that […]

entries.title: NASA Award Boosts Space Technology Research Capabilities
entries.description: NASA is introducing a new funding opportunity to accelerate academic research and technology development. The Minority University Research and Education Project Space Technology Artemis Research (M‑STAR) application window opened Thursday and will remain open through 11:59 p.m. EDT on Tuesday, Aug. 11. The research funded through this award supports the agency’s priorities for exploring the […]

entries.title: World Cup Fever in Guadalajara
entries.description: The city’s metro area has pushed westward since it last hosted World Cup matches in 1986, expanding across a landscape shaped by ancient volcanoes.

entries.title: Venus and Jupiter: Conjunction from Avebury
entries.description:
To see Venus and Jupiter
together this month, you won't need binoculars or even a telescope

entries.title: Hubble Sees Swarm of Galaxies
entries.description: Looking somewhat like a swarm of bees returning to their hive, this NASA Hubble Space Telescope image features the galaxy cluster MACS0329-0211.

entries.title: Black Eye Galaxy
entries.description: This March 20, 2026, image of Messier 64, or the Black Eye Galaxy, is a composite view from NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope and James Webb Space Telescope. It shows Messier 64 captured at near- and mid-infrared wavelengths by Webb, while Hubble’s image shows the galaxy in ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared light. Messier 64 is characterized […]

entries.title: NASA to Cover 34th SpaceX Resupply Mission Space Station Departure
entries.description: NASA and its international partners are set to receive scientific research samples and hardware as a SpaceX Dragon spacecraft is scheduled to depart the International Space Station on Tuesday, June 16, for its return to Earth. Watch NASA’s live undocking coverage beginning at 11:45 a.m. EDT on NASA+, Amazon Prime, and the agency’s YouTube channel. […]

entries.title: Botany and Biology Research Wrap Up Week as Dragon Preps for Earth Return
entries.description: Space agriculture, stem cells, and cargo operations wrapped up the week aboard the International Space Station. The Expedition 74 crew also conducted blood tests and installed a new food processor as a U.S. cargo spacecraft nears the end of its mission. The post Botany and Biology Research Wrap Up Week as Dragon Preps for Earth Return appeared first on NASA Science .

entries.title: Hazard Detection Lidar System Goes to the Moon
entries.description: Enabling landers to autonomously identify the safest sites to touch down Moon craters and their surrounding areas are high-value destinations for lunar missions since they may contain important resources and valuable science targets. However, these sites are also home to dangers such as steep slopes that are difficult to see in the challenging lighting conditions. […] The post Hazard Detection Lidar System Goes to the Moon appeared first on NASA Science .

entries.title: Join Us at the 248th AAS Meeting, June 2026
entries.description: Join us at the 248th AAS Meeting in Pasadena, 14 - 18 June, 2026 The post Join Us at the 248th AAS Meeting, June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entries.title: NASA-Funded Research Follows Bird Flight; Birds Follow Their Noses
entries.description: You might think birds skimming over the ocean wouldn’t seek wind unless it was pushing them in the right direction, but NASA-funded researchers have learned that storm petrels find stiff crosswinds worth the slowdown, in return for the clues and cues the gusts carry. In a paper published by the Royal Society’s Biology Letters May 13, researchers from Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution (WHOI) and the Italian Institute for […] The post NASA-Funded Research Follows Bird Flight; Birds Follow Their

entries.title: NASA’s X-59 Reaches Speed, Altitude for Future Quiet Supersonic Flights
entries.description: NASA’s X-59 experimental aircraft reached a major milestone Friday, June 12, flying Mach 1.4 (about 924 mph) and an altitude of 55,000 feet, the conditions required for the aircraft to make future flights critical to its mission.   The X-59 still has months of performance testing ahead, but after those are complete, NASA’s Quesst mission will fly the aircraft over several U.S. communities to collect data on public perception of the quiet sonic thump it will make at […] The post NASA’s X-59 Reach

entries.title: PhysCOS Activities at AAS 248, 14–15 June 2026
entries.description: If you’re attending the American Astronomical Society’s 2026 Summer Meeting (AAS 248) in Pasadena CA, please join us for  PhysCOS-organized activities. The post PhysCOS Activities at AAS 248, 14–15 June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .
